figures.caption: Figure 2 | 𝜏2-Bench accuracy over RL training. 𝒟𝑎𝑑𝑣 yields the strongest optimization and outperform same-data SFT.
Figure 3 | 𝜏2-Bench reward standard deviation during RL training. 𝒟𝑎𝑑𝑣 sets maintain higher reward variance and therefore more informative local updates.
Table 6 shows a monotonic benefit from more selective filtering: random pivots already improve over SFT (59.68 vs. 58.44), and low-reward-mean pivots yield the best result (63.81).

Figure 1 | SWE-Bench accuracy vs. (a) cumulative rollout turns and (b) cumulative rollout time during training, using the same number of compute nodes. PivotRL reaches comparable accuracy to E2E RL with ∼4× fewer rollout turns and ∼5.5× less wall-clock time.
Table 2 | OOD performance change (Δ) relative to Base, averaged across the four training runs in Table 1. SFT degrades by −9.83 on average; PivotRL maintains near-zero change (+0.21).

Table 3 | Per-domain OOD breakdown across four single-domain training runs. Each cell shows score followed by Δ relative to Base. PivotRL (rl) consistently preserves OOD performance, whereas SFT (sft) causes broad regression—particularly after terminal-domain training.
